政治经济学的现状：政治形态与法律力量

引言

在传统的马克思主义学术中，国家通常被视为要么是由经济决定的实体，要么是代表通过国家作为其利益的公共权威主张自己的主导社会力量的权力。在后一种观点中，国家出现为推进霸权项目的战略舞台。在前一种看法中，国家出现为经济决定的上层建筑。这两种观点都没有将国家作为资产阶级社会的政治形态加以发展。遵循布劳恩斯坦的观点，阿多诺的批判理论中几乎没有关于国家概念性的论证。新的马克思解读与20世纪70年代所谓的德国国家衍生论辩论重叠，但除了赖歇尔特以外，一个讨论了价值的概念性，另一个试图从资本主义经济范畴的运动中衍生出国家的角色和功能。"衍生"一词简明扼要地总结了这场辩论的主导论述。这场辩论没有建立国家作为社会政治形态的概念性，而是将国家衍生为资本主义积累过程的政治补充。这些论述都没有从实际的、既定的生活关系中发展出国家，因此没有提供作为特定社会关系的神圣化形式的国家的批判。

马克思对国家的批判，只存在于碎片中，表明除了他关于经济基础和政治上层建筑的论证外，他还将国家看作资本主义社会关系的政治形态。事实上，正如我在下文中辩论的，国家是从社会的经济基础中产生的政治上层建筑的观念源自古典政治经济学。在他对政治经济学的批判中，这是一个矛盾的观点。他的工作旨在批判经济客观性的关系，如果认真对待，这将削弱国家据说产生的那个经济基础。只有一个社会现实，那就是以资本主义组织的生产社会关系的现实。“我们生活的世界之外没有其他世界。”因此，这一章认为，国家最好被概念化为资本主义社会的政治形态，而不是从一些假定的社会经济范畴中衍生出来，无论是来自多种竞争的霸权战略、不可抗拒的经济逻辑，还是作为资本主义积累过程的功能要求。

本章采取的方法代表了一个转变。它不是通过马克思自己关于国家的著作碎片和马克思主义文献中对国家批判的既定元素来发展其论点，而是通过探索黑格尔的政治哲学、史密斯的政治经济学，最后是新自由主义国家理论，尤其是德国新自由主义思想，它为将资本主义经济作为政府实践建立了一个连贯的论述。最后一节讨论新自由主义作为国家的政治神学，为现代波拿巴主义的理论阐述提供了基础。我认为这种方法上的转变以直接的方式确立了本章的论点，即政治世界是以政治形态出现的社会世界。我在结论中回到马克思的论述，以展示他对国家作为“以国家形态集中的资产阶级社会”的概念的批判力量。在这种观点下，社会分为社会和国家，即国家是“关于自身的社会的政治形态”。因此，我认为国家和社会的机构分离并没有赋予国家独立的政治构成和发展逻辑。相反，政治国家是非强制性、非政治化的劳动力买卖者与剩余价值生产者之间交换关系的前提，尽管他们表现出明显的不平等，但他们基于法治追求自己的利益，作为平等的法律主体，这种追求表达了阶级斗争的历史。因此，本章认为，国家不是某种超经济力量。相反，它是“社会的集中和组织化的力量”。

关于社会的政治形态

黑格尔将资产阶级社会设想为具有对立性质的。正是由于其对立的特性，它需要一个政治形态。他从资产阶级社会的内在特性发展出国家的必要性。在他的《法哲学》中，他首先引入了普遍依赖的概念：特定个人本质上与其他特定个人相关，每个人通过其他人找到满足，他们的相互关系意味着需要满足构成了一个普遍的相互依赖系统。因此存在分工和通过交换满足需要。关于国家，其目的在于通过司法行政“保护财产”。然而，重要的是，“无限复杂、交错的相互生产和交换的运动，以及其中使用的同样无限多样的手段”超越了将社会划分为个体化的个体，或用今天的语言来说，所谓的市场个体。然后他认为，分工凝结为“系统，个体被分配到其中的一个或另一个系统中——换句话说，分成阶级”。这些分裂在性质上是对立的，因为资产阶级社会的发展导致其极化为对立的阶级关系。 根据黑格尔的观点，社会分裂为两个对立阶级是资产阶级社会的内在必然性。它属于其构成动力。正如他所看到的那样，资产阶级社会“导致与[工作]相关的阶级的依赖和困境”。 依赖和困境也包含在“无法感受和享受民间社会更广泛的自由，尤其是知识方面的好处”的无能中。此外，资产阶级社会的扩大再生产“导致穷人的暴民的产生”和“少数人手中集中过多的财富”。当大众开始陷入贫困并开始反抗时该怎么办？他拒绝重新分配财富，因为这“会违反民间社会的原则”。他也拒绝了当今所谓的充分就业政策，因为这与其逻辑相悖。这不仅不能解决问题，反而会加剧问题。因此，“尽管财富过剩，民间社会还是不够富裕，即自身资源不足以抑制过度的贫困和穷人的暴民的产生”。社会的极化没有经济答案。经济既不能提供秩序，也不能抑制“暴民”。实际上，“民间社会的内在辩证法……驱使它……超越自身的界限”。如何将阶级对立保持在其资产阶级形态的界限内？对于黑格尔来说，只有一个政治答案。他将国家视为资产阶级社会的政治力量，并指责它包含阶级对立。

黑格尔将资产阶级社会设想为在其社会性质上完全不文明。他将其描绘为自私、竞争和对立的特性。它允许少数人通过牺牲众多人积累巨大财富，使大众陷入贫困。黑格尔如下描述资产阶级社会：

[个体]受到整体的完全混乱和风险的支配。大量人口被迫从事工厂、制造业、矿山等令人麻木、不健康和不安全的劳动。整个行业支撑着大量人口，却因为模式变化或因为其他国家的新发明导致其产品价值下降，或因为其他原因而突然倒闭。大量人群因此被抛弃至无助的贫困之中。巨大的财富和巨大的贫困之间的冲突显现出来，这种贫困无法改善自身的条件。财富...成为一种主导力量[,激发怨恨和憎恨]。

他因此将资产阶级社会描述为“行尸走肉的活动生命。这一系统盲目而原始地四处移动，就像一头野兽需要强大的持久控制和遏制”。简而言之，它需要国家的权威使其行为“文明化”，通过将与工作相关的阶级保持在资产阶级社会的界限内。事实上，通过“成功的战争”来维持社会的凝聚力可能是有益的，这些战争“遏制了国内的动荡并巩固了国家在国内的权力”。没有政治国家，资产阶级社会将在自身重量下内爆。其存在的安全、社会秩序和文明行为，是国家的事务。

政治经济学的状态：关于警察，正义和道德情操

马克思在引入其基础/上层建筑隐喻时争论说，他的调查使他得出结论，生产关系“构成了社会的经济结构，真正的基础，上面产生了法律和政治上层建筑，并且对应于特定形式的社会意识。”撇开马克思自己对自己的工作的理解，即作为对经济范畴的批判，从而对据说上层建筑产生的经济客观性，他的隐喻表明资产阶级社会的政治形态，即国家，属于它所源自的社会。粗略地说，资本的目的是积累提取的剩余价值，而国家是那个目的的政治形态。基础/上层建筑隐喻的起源在于古典政治经济学。威廉·罗伯逊很好地总结了古典立场：“在关于人们在社会中联合起来的操作的每一个调查中，首先关注的对象应该是他们的生存方式。根据生存方式的不同，他们的法律和政策必须有所不同。”亚当·斯密提供了古典阐述。他的历史理论因其对经济力量的重视而引人注目，这些力量通过历史发展向着“商业社会”迈进。他认为，在每个历史阶段，社会的政治形态，无论是以权威还是管辖权的形式构想，都必然源自产权形式。对于斯密来说，私有财产是分工发展的结果，他认为，“这不是人类智慧的效果，而是交易和易货倾向的缓慢和逐渐的后果”。交换的内在动力通过其自身的力量导致了越来越大的分工，分工的扩展增加了社会剩余，并导致社会向不同的社会阶层进行日益增长的社会分化，从而导致私有财产的建立和社会在商业社会中分化为民间社会和政治国家。

请将“For Adam Smith political economy is not an economic science. Rather, it is ‘a branch of the science of the statesman or legislator’.23 He, too, conceived of what he called ‘commercial society’ as class ridden, and held the state indispensable for maintaining the system of perfect liberty. It maintains this system by means not only of law but also by ‘ordering’ the conduct of society, restraining the passions of competition within the framework of the moral sentiments and law, and containing the class antagonism. The invisible hand is incapable of integrating and maintaining society. It does not remove the social impediments to its operation, nor does it create the moral sentiments upon which the conduct between the private interests depends, and nor does it resolve the clashes of interest between the classes. That is, the invisible hand has no independent reality. Fundamentally, it is a practice of government. Economy is political economy.

According to Smith, production and distribution are regulated through competitive exchanges, mediated by money. This regulation is achieved where there is ‘perfect liberty’. The price mechanism that allows prices to rise and fall above and below the ‘natural prices’ is governed by the invisible hand, which informs individuals where to invest and what to sell. The invisible hand is a depoliticized devise of economic adjustment. Individuals follow price signals in a manner of their own choosing and in pursuit of their own interests. Government or persons do not tell anybody what to do, when and where. Nevertheless, its magic requires government. Impediments to its operation have to be removed by means of state, and economic freedom requires the order of freedom to sustain its sociability, preventing disorder. The Smithean state is therefore not a weak state. It is a strong state. It does not yield to the social interests. It governs over them, and as I argue below, also through them to secure the perfect liberty of the system as a whole. That is, for Smith the state does not compete with the invisible hand as if it were some alternative source of economic regulation. Rather, it is to facilitate its unimpeded operation, and it therefore governs for the sake of the system of perfect liberty. Its purpose is to protect, maintain and facilitate the law of private property to secure the further development of commercial society. Smith specifies a number of indispensable state functions. Apart from defending the country against external threats, it has to provide for an exact administration of justice in order to resolve clashes of interest between property owners. The state is indispensable also for the provision of public goods that are essential for the operation of the market, but which cannot be provided for by the market itself for lack of profitability.24 This responsibility for commercial society also entails that the state make efforts to help achieve the ‘cheapness of provision’, facilitating the progressive development of the wealth of nations on the basis of increased labour productivity.25 The state, then, is responsible for facilitating the law of private property, or as Marx called it, the law of value, by removing various institutional and legal impediments, and by confronting those private interests that impede the perfect liberty of the market by advancing their egoistic interests or indeed class interests.

For Smith, ‘justice . . . is the main pillar that holds up the whole edifice’ of commercial society.26 Justice is a matter not only of law and the rule of law, which is the regulative force of the freedom of contract between ostensibly equal exchange subjects,27 fundamentally, justice is a matter of the well-ordered ‘common wealth’. Order is the precondition of law. The rule of law does not apply to social ‘disorder and bloodshed’, and law does not enforce order. Police is responsible for the establishment of order and police is therefore the premise of the rule of law. Police enforces order and in this manner renders the rule of law effective. Further, the system of justice is dependent upon a moral code that commits individuals to the rules of justice, and therewith to the laws of private property. The removal of impediments to market freedom thus also entails the provision of morally committed participants in market freedom. That is, the system of perfect liberty amounts to a constant effort of restraining the passions of competition and profit-making by the rules of justice. It amounts also to a constant effort of facilitating ‘the will’ for competition and enterprise on the part of the class divided social individuals. There is thus need to facilitate the moral sentiments of the system of perfect liberty in ‘the will’ of the individuals, contain the passions of ‘self-love’, curb the rebellious character of the poor and facilitate the further improvements in the productive power of labour to sustain the progress of the common wealth.

According to Smith, the moral sentiments of commercial society are based on the sense of the ‘propriety’ of the beauty of a well-ordered whole. This whole gives purpose and benefit to the private individuals who are both interested only in themselves and obliged to each other. The moral sentiments therefore express a moral sociability, which for Smith is based on ‘sympathy’. Sympathy is the ability of individuals to adopt the position of the ‘impartial and well-informed spectator’. However, sympathy is not a sufficient condition for containing the fundamental character of commercial society, that is, ‘self-love’. A society based on the pursuit of self-interest requires a moral foundation, a moral social fabric and an ethical framework, to sustain it. On the part of the self-interested individuals, the moral sentiments express, first, the charitable side of commercial society, or as Marx put it in the Holy Family, its sentimentality.28 Inasmuch as the concept of ‘free labour contains the pauper’, the character trait of private property combines free competition and robust enforcement of labour discipline on the factory floor, stealing atoms of additional labour time, with an impulse to charity for the poor and downtrodden.29 Second, for Smith, the moral sentiments manifest the mentality of the system of liberty. Their provision belongs therefore to the state, which renders the moral sentiments as the well-informed power of commercial society. That is, for Smith the political state is not really an impartial and well-informed spectator. Rather, it is charged with making the system of perfect liberty valid in the mentality of society.30 In short, the Smithean state is charged with rendering the system of perfect liberty effective. For Smith, the state governs in the true interests of the common wealth, appealing to and connecting with the sociable core of self-interested individuals, restraining their immediate individual interests and class interests within a moral framework that both legitimizes the system of justice and embeds the morality of private property, sympathy and competitiveness into the inner recess of the acting individuals, securing the physiognomy of bourgeois society.

For the sake of liberty it is thus necessary to employ ‘the power of the commonwealth’, that is, the state, ‘to enforce the practice of justice. Without this precaution, commercial society will descend into bloodshed and disorder, every man revenging himself at his own hand whenever he fancied he is injured.’ Punishment is the condition of justice. ‘All men delight’ to see injustice ‘punished’, and injustice needs to ‘be punished . . . on account of the order of society’.31 Only those who do not violate the laws of justice are ‘left perfectly free to pursue [their] own interests [their] own way, and to bring both [their] industry and capital into competition with any other man, or order of man’.32 He thus suggests that the state is responsible for securing the proper use of freedom – by means of police: it punishes the misuse of freedom and thus enforces the order of freedom, securing the law of private property. That is, the ‘affluence of the rich excites the indignation of the poor, who are often both driven by want, and promoted by envy to invade his possessions’. The freedom of private property therefore ‘necessarily requires the establishment of civil government’ to contain the ‘indignance of the many’. That is, ‘civil government, so far as it is instituted for the security of property, is in reality instituted for the defence of the rich against the poor, or of those who have some property against those who have none at all’.33 However, for Smith the defence of private property against the poor is ostensibly undertaken in the interest of the poor as it is only by means of the system of perfect liberty that the poor will benefit from the law of private property as wealth will eventually trickle down.

Smith introduces the class struggle between capital and labour arguing that ‘wages depend upon contract between two parties whose interests are not the same’. That is, the ‘workmen desire to get a lot, the master to give as little as possible. The former are disposed to combine to raise, the latter to lower the wages of labour.’ In this struggle, the masters have the upper hand because they ‘are fewer in number, and combine much more easily; they can live for longer without getting their profits, the workers are starved’. That workers rebel is understandable given their ‘desperate conditions’. Yet, their action is foolish because ‘the masters react with purpose and force the worker back and that is, the workmen very seldom derive any advantage from the violence of those tumultuous combinations’.34 The only way to raise wages and improve conditions is by sustained accumulation, which increases the demand for labour and thus improves the market position of the worker. Resolution of the workers’ desperate conditions and quarrelsome nature does not lie in the admittedly unequal exchange relation between capital and labour. Resolution lies in the dynamic increase in wages that depend on the most rapid growth possible in the demand for labour, which results from the growth of the market, increase in trade and commerce, which is based on the division of labour, which in turn is fed by greater labour productivity. Therefore, ‘workers do well not to struggle, because with the increase of surplus, stock accumulates, increasing the number of workers, and the increase of revenue and stock is the increase of national wealth. The demand for those who live by wages . . . increases with the increase in national wealth.’ This, then, is the famous trickle-down effect – accumulation, he argues, increases national wealth and ‘occasions a rise in the wage of labour’. Smith calls this the ‘liberal reward for labour’, and one consequence of his argument is, of course, that if there are poor, then this is an indication that ‘things are at a stand’, requiring state action to facilitate ‘the cheapness of goods of all sorts’ by means of increased labour productivity as the foundation of the profitability of stock, ensuring the price competitiveness of the products of labour in a world governed by the harsh reality of the invisible hand.35”翻译为中文

对亚当·斯密而言，政治经济学不是一门经济学科。相反，它是“政治家或立法者科学的一个分支”。他同样将所谓的“商业社会”视为阶级社会，并认为国家对于维持完美自由的体系是不可或缺的。国家不仅通过法律手段维持这一体系，还通过“规范”社会行为，将竞争的激情限制在道德情感和法律的框架内，并包含阶级对立。看不见的手无法整合和维护社会。它无法消除其运作的社会障碍，也无法创造私人利益之间行为所依赖的道德情感，也无法解决阶级之间的利益冲突。也就是说，看不见的手没有独立的现实。根本上，它是一种政府实践。经济是政治经济学。

根据斯密的说法，生产和分配通过竞争性交换进行调节，由货币中介。这种调节在“完美自由”的条件下实现。允许价格在“自然价格”之上和之下上升和下降的价格机制由看不见的手控制，它告知个体在何处投资和销售何物。看不见的手是经济调节的去政治化装置。个体按照他们自己的选择和为了自己的利益追随价格信号。政府或个人并不告诉任何人该做什么，何时何地做。然而，其魔力需要政府的支持。必须通过国家手段消除对其运作的阻碍，经济自由需要自由秩序的支持，以防止混乱。因此，斯密式的国家不是一个软弱的国家。它是一个强大的国家。它不向社会利益屈服。它统治着它们，正如我在下文中辩论的，也通过它们来确保整个体系的完美自由。也就是说，对于斯密来说，国家并不是与看不见的手竞争，就好像它是某种经济调节的替代来源。相反，它是为了促进其无阻碍的运作，因此它为了完美自由的体系而治理。其目的是保护、维持和促进私有财产法律，以确保商业社会的进一步发展。斯密指定了一些不可或缺的国家职能。除了防御外部威胁外，它还必须提供精确的司法管理，以解决产权所有者之间的利益冲突。国家对于提供市场运作所必需但市场本身因缺乏盈利性而无法提供的公共产品也是不可或缺的。这种对商业社会的责任还包括国家努力帮助实现“物资供应的便宜”，通过提高劳动生产率促进国家财富的进步发展。因此，国家负责促进私有财产法律，或如马克思所说的，价值法律，通过消除各种制度和法律障碍，并对那些通过推进他们自私的利益或确实是阶级利益来阻碍市场完美自由的私人利益进行对抗。

对于斯密来说，“正义……是支撑商业社会整个建筑的主要支柱”。正义不仅是法律和法治的问题，法治是明显平等的交换主体之间自由合同的调节力量，从根本上讲，正义是有序的“共同财富”的问题。秩序是法律的前提条件。法治不适用于社会“混乱和流血”，法律也不强制执行秩序。警察负责建立秩序，因此警察是法治的前提。警察执行秩序，以这种方式使法治有效。此外，司法系统依赖于将个体承诺于正义规则的道德准则，进而承诺于私有财产法律。因此，消除市场自由的障碍也意味着需要提供道德上致力于市场自由的参与者。也就是说，完美自由的体系相当于不断努力通过正义的规则抑制竞争和赚钱的激情。它还相当于不断努力促使被社会阶级分化的个体“意志”中进行竞争和企业。因此，需要在个体的“意志”中促进完美自由体系的道德情感，遏制“自爱”的激情，抑制贫困者的叛逆性格，并促进劳动生产力的进一步提高，以维持共同财富的进步。

根据斯密的说法，商业社会的道德情感基于对整体良好秩序之美的“适当性”的感知。这个整体赋予了只对自己感兴趣且彼此有义务的私人个体目的和利益。因此，道德情感表达了一种道德社会性，对于斯密来说，这基于“同情”。同情是个体采取“公正和知情的旁观者”立场的能力。然而，同情不是遏制商业社会的基本特征，即“自私”，的充分条件。一个基于追求自身利益的社会需要道德基础、道德社会结构和伦理框架来维持。对于自利的个体而言，道德情感首先表达了商业社会的慈善方面，或如马克思在《圣家》中所说的，其感伤性。因为“自由劳动包含着贫困者”，私有财产的性格特征结合了自由竞争和工厂车间中劳动纪律的严格执行，偷取额外的劳动时间，以及对穷人和受压迫者的慈善冲动。其次，对于斯密来说，道德情感体现了自由体系的心态。因此，其提供属于国家，国家使道德情感成为商业社会的知情力量。也就是说，对于斯密来说，政治国家并不真的是一个公正和知情的旁观者。相反，它被赋予了使完美自由体系在社会心态中有效的任务。简而言之，斯密式国家被赋予了使完美自由体系有效的任务。对于斯密来说，国家在真正的共同财富利益中治理，呼吁并连接着自私个体的社会核心，抑制他们直接的个人利益和阶级利益，置于既合法化正义体系又将私有财产、同情和竞争性嵌入行动个体内心深处的道德框架内，确保资产阶级社会的面貌。

为了自由，因此有必要使用“共同体的力量”，即国家，“强制执行正义的实践。没有这个预防措施，商业社会将沦为流血和混乱，每个人都用自己的手为自己报仇，每当他觉得自己受到了伤害。”惩罚是正义的条件。“所有人都乐于”看到不公“受到惩罚”，并且不公必须“因为社会秩序的缘故而受到惩罚”。只有那些不违反正义法律的人“完全自由地按照[自己的]方式追求[自己的]利益，将[自己的]产业和资本与任何其他人或人群的资本进行竞争”。他因此暗示国家负责确保自由的正确使用——通过警察手段：它惩罚自由的滥用，从而强制执行自由的秩序，确保私有财产法律。也就是说，“富人的富裕激起了穷人的愤怒，穷人常常因为贫困和嫉妒而入侵他的财产”。因此，私有财产的自由“必然要求建立民事政府”以包容“众多的愤怒”。也就是说，“民事政府，就其为保护财产安全而设立的范围而言，实际上是为了保护富人免受穷人的侵害，或者保护那些拥有一些财产的人免受那些根本没有财产的人的侵害”。然而，对于斯密来说，保护私有财产免受穷人侵害表面上是为了穷人的利益，因为只有通过完美自由的体系，穷人才能从私有财产法律中受益，因为财富最终会向下渗透。

斯密引入了资本和劳动之间的阶级斗争，争论说“工资取决于两方之间的合同，而这两方的利益并不相同”。也就是说，“工人希望得到很多，主人希望尽可能少给。前者倾向于结合以提高工资，后者倾向于降低工资。”在这场斗争中，主人占上风，因为他们“人数较少，更容易结合；他们可以更长时间地不获取利润，工人则会挨饿”。工人反抗是可以理解的，鉴于他们的“绝望条件”。然而，他们的行动是愚蠢的，因为“主人带着目的和力量做出反应，迫使工人退缩，也就是说，工人从这些动荡的结合中很少获得任何好处。”提高工资和改善条件的唯一方法是通过持续的积累，这增加了对劳动的需求，从而改善了工人的市场地位。解决工人绝望条件和好斗性质的问题并不在于资本和劳动之间不平等的交换关系中。解决问题在于依赖于对劳动需求最快速增长的工资动态提高，这取决于市场增长、贸易和商业增加，这基于分工，反过来又依赖于更大的劳动生产率。因此，“工人最好不要斗争，因为随着剩余的增加，库存积累，增加了工人的数量，收入和库存的增加就是国家财富的增加。随着国家财富的增加，依靠工资生活的人的需求……也随之增加。”这就是著名的向下渗透效应——斯密争论说，积累增加了国家财富并“导致了劳动工资的上涨”。斯密称之为“劳动的慷慨奖励”，他的论点的一个后果当然是，如果有穷人，那么这表明“事情停滞不前”，需要国家采取行动通过增加劳动生产率来促进“各类商品的便宜”，以确保库存盈利性的基础上，确保劳动产品在由看不见的手控制的严酷现实的世界中的价格竞争力。

请将“The owners of stock in some countries might achieve higher rates of return on their investment than owners in some other countries, ‘which no doubt demonstrate[s] the redundancy of their stock’. For their stock to be maintained, competitive adjustment is required. Adjustment is neither automatic nor assured. It requires facilitation, which belongs to the state. The state is responsible for the progress of the common wealth. Indeed, police is needed to secure the system of perfect liberty in the face of ignorant and quarrelsome workers and the profit-seeking owners of stock. For Smith, while ‘national wealth’ and ‘workers’ benefit from progressive accumulation, the owners of stock might not because ‘the increase in stock, which raises wage, tends to lower profit’.36 Capitalists, he suggests, pursue their own narrow class interests and might therefore seek to maintain the rate of profit artificially, impeding the natural liberty of the market, for example, by means of monopoly, price fixing or protectionism. In this context, too, the state acts to enforce the system of perfect liberty. That is, ‘whatever regulations are made with respect to the trade, commerce, agriculture, manufactures of the country are considered as belonging to the police’.37 Effective policing entails a strong state, a state where it belongs: over and above the egoistic interests and class struggles, ostensibly not governing in the interest of either but in the interest of the beauty of the well-ordered whole of the system of liberty, securing its propriety. The state thus governs in the interests of the bonum comune of commercial society. It intervenes in the behaviour of individuals to restrain their passions that are governed by ‘self-love’ and short-term class interests to secure its further development. Concerning the poor, police is needed to make the worker accept that ‘if he is frugal and industrious, [he] may enjoy a greater share of the necessaries and conveniences of life than it is possible for any savage to acquire’. There is thus need, also, for ‘the instruction of the people’ chiefly by means of education and public diversions.38 For Smith, then, government should take pains to offset the socially and morally destructive effects of accumulation, by assuming responsibility for cultural activities to render society, in its moral sentiments, economic behaviour and social relations.

The administration of justice, which secures the law of private property and gives ‘the inhabitants of a country liberty and security’, describes, in Smith’s words, an order of good government that enforces the system of perfect liberty upon the owners of stock to contain their passion within the framework of law, order, morality and sociability. For the benefit of everybody in society, including the poor who depend for the improvement of their circumstances on the increase in wealth, the political state enforces the discipline of a rules-based competitive system to secure the further division of labour based on improved labour productivity. The purpose of the state is thus to secure for the invisible hand that perfect order upon which the progress of commercial society depends. For the sake of progress, the dynamic of commercial society needs to be sustained, and in this context, the ‘inequality in the fortunes of mankind’ is in fact ‘useful’.39

In conclusion, the state maintains ‘the rich in the possession of their wealth against the violence and rapacity of the poor’ and it does so in the interest of the poor themselves.40 Wealth will eventually trickle down to allow for that improvement of conditions which only economic growth can furnish. He thus conceives of the state unashamedly as a class state that, ostensibly, operates in the true interest of workers – for economic growth, security of employment and wage income and conditions. For Smith, the workers do well not to resist the progressive accumulation of capital inasmuch as their very existence depends on it. The base superstructure metaphor that Marx derived from classical political economy focuses the state as the political force of the law of value.41 As a tax state, it depends entirely on the progressive accumulation of the wealth of the nation, and thus on the international competitiveness of ‘stock’. However, the class character of the state is not defined in national terms. It subsists through world-market relations. As Smith put it,

the proprietor of stock is properly a citizen of the world, and is not necessarily attached to any particular country. He would be apt to abandon the country in which he was exposed to a vexatious inquisition, in order to be assessed to a burdensome tax, and would remove his stock to some other country where he could either carry on his business, or enjoy his fortune more at his ease.42

In the well-ordered common wealth, the law of private property manifests itself in the form of the heavy artillery of the price mechanism, which feeds on the ‘cheapness of provision’ and which thus entails constant pressure to achieve greater labour productivity, the facilitation of which ‘produces what we call police’.43





Neoliberalism and the theory of the state: On free economy and market police

The conventional view that neoliberalism has to do with the weakening of the state has little, if anything, to do with the neoliberal conception of the free economy. For the neoliberals, too, the state is the indispensable political force of the society of free labour.44 Laissez-faire is no ‘answer to riots’.45 For the neoliberals the issue is not whether the state should or should not intervene. Rather, the issue is the purpose and method, the objective and aim of state intervention. Regulation by the invisible hand is indispensable – in the economic sphere. Left to its own devices, market freedom will ‘degenerate into a vulgar brawl’ (Röpke) between the ‘greedy self-seekers’ and implode in the face of the destructive forces of the proletariat (Rüstow).46 It ‘does not breed social integration’ nor does it create the moral sentiment and entrepreneurial commitments that sustain bourgeois society.47 The free economy is a practice of government. In the words of Hayek, the state is indispensable as the ‘economic planner for competition’.48 It facilitates (free) economy as a politically ordered freedom, which extends beyond the system of justice and the rule of law. Fundamentally economic freedom depends on ‘the will’ (Böhm) for economic freedom, which requires the ‘incorporation [of competition] into a total life-style’ relieving, says Müller-Armack, workers from the fear of capitalist freedom and making them accept self-responsibility for that freedom.49 The state is thus required to secure the ‘psycho-moral forces’ at the disposal of a capitalist society, transforming rebellious proletarians into self-responsible entrepreneurs of labour power.50

Neoliberalism recognizes that free economy is not some automatic process, which unfolds or asserts itself objectively over the acting individuals. Capitalist economy does not comprise ‘unconditionally valid economic laws’ nor is capitalism governed by the theological quirks of the invisible hand, nor do the dependent masses accept their existence as propertyless producers of surplus value without a fight.51 The circumstance that the labourers are ‘governed’ by the dull compulsion of economic need does neither come about just like that, nor does it maintain itself just like that. Its reality is neither given nor assured. It is made. That is, the magic of the invisible hand depends on uninhibited market forces, which in turn depend on the successful removal of impediments to free economy by the order-making and preserving state. Laissez-faire describes the wished-for economic conditions. It does not extend to the political sphere. In fact, Hayek argues that laissez-faire is ‘a highly ambiguous and misleading description of the principles on which a liberal policy is based’.52 Prevention of the ‘coercion and violence’ by private powers, principally by organized labour and self-organization of quarrelsome proletarians, is a political task.53 It aims at the ‘complete eradication of all orderlessness from markets and the elimination of private power from the economy’,54 securing the social individuals as rational actors of economic value, mere human material for the valorization of value. The institutional separation of the state from the economy is not just a condition of economic liberty and social freedom, of the depoliticized exchange relations between the commodity owners. Rather, economic liberty is a practice of government. The depoliticization of the social relations is not a product of economic liberty. In its entirety it amounts to a practice of government. Free economy presupposes the absence of direct coercion; the buyers of labour power and producers of surplus value make contracts as free and equal citizens. Thus the free economy entails depoliticized relations based on a contract between self-responsible economic agents. Direct coercion and violence is replaced by economic preferences, that is, in reality, the silent force of economic compulsion. This organization of the exchange relations requires the power of the state as the force of socio-economic depoliticization. The trade relations in labour power and its effective utility as a means of greater wealth amounts to a political practice of depoliticized socio-economic relations. That is, free economy ‘is an eminently political decision’, which needs to be made time and time again to contain the illiberal use of freedom and prevent the politicization of the social labour relations.55 The Smithean notion that the political state is innate to the concept of the invisible hand is fundamental to neoliberal thought. It sees the state as the public authority of the invisible hand, which is the regulative means of the depoliticized relations of factor competiveness and labour utility. Concentrated and organized coercive force is thus the precondition of free economy. The laws of equivalent exchange are premised on order, and the establishment of order is a political matter. That is, the invisible hand is neither an answer ‘to the hungry hordes of vested interests’, nor is it an answer to the rapacity of the poor, as Smith put it.56 The invisible hand is premised on the state as ‘market police’.57 Market police amounts to a constant effort at securing the social and moral preconditions for free economy. That is, in its entirety economic regulation by the invisible hand is a political practice.”翻译为中文

在一些国家中，股份所有者可能会获得比其他国家的所有者更高的投资回报率，“这无疑说明了他们的股份过剩”。为了维持他们的股份，需要竞争调整。调整既不是自动的，也不是有保证的。这需要国家的便利化。国家负责共同财富的进步。确实，需要警察来确保面对无知和好争吵的工人以及寻求利润的股份所有者时的完美自由体系。对于斯密来说，尽管“国家财富”和“工人”受益于持续积累，但股份所有者可能不会受益，因为“增加的库存，提高了工资，往往会降低利润”。他暗示，资本家追求自己狭窄的阶级利益，因此可能试图人为地维持利润率，妨碍市场的自然自由，例如，通过垄断、定价或保护主义。在这种情况下，国家也采取行动来执行完美自由的体系。也就是说，“关于国家的贸易、商业、农业、制造业的任何规定都被视为属于警察范畴。”有效的警察行动需要一个强大的国家，一个属于它应有之处的国家：在自私的利益和阶级斗争之上，表面上不是为了任何一方的利益而治理，而是为了完美有序整体之美的利益而治理，确保其适当性。因此，国家在商业社会的共同利益下治理。它干预个人行为以抑制由“自爱”和短期阶级利益支配的激情，以确保其进一步发展。关于穷人，需要警察让工人接受“如果他节俭和勤奋，[他]可能享有比任何野蛮人都更多的生活必需品和便利”。因此，还需要“对人民的教育”，主要通过教育和公共娱乐。因此，对于斯密来说，政府应该努力抵消积累的社会和道德破坏效应，通过承担文化活动的责任，使社会在其道德情感、经济行为和社会关系上得以体现。

行政司法，确保私有财产法律并给予“一个国家的居民自由和安全”，用斯密的话来描述，是一个良好政府秩序，它赋予股份所有者完美自由的体系，将他们的激情包含在法律、秩序、道德和社交的框架之内。为了社会中每个人的利益，包括那些依赖于财富增加来改善他们境况的穷人，政治国家执行基于改进劳动生产率的进一步分工所需的基于规则的竞争体系的纪律。因此，国家的目的是为看不见的手确保那种完美的秩序，商业社会的进步依赖于这种秩序。为了进步，商业社会的动态需要得到维持，而在这种情况下，“人类财富的不平等实际上是有用的”。

总之，国家“维护富人免受穷人的暴力和掠夺”，并且这样做是为了穷人自己的利益。财富最终会向下渗透，以实现只有经济增长才能提供的条件改善。因此，他毫不掩饰地将国家设想为一个阶级国家，这个国家表面上是为了工人的真正利益而运作——为了经济增长、就业安全、工资收入和条件而运作。对于斯密来说，工人不反抗资本的持续积累是明智的，因为他们的生存完全依赖于它。马克思从古典政治经济学中衍生出的基础/上层建筑隐喻聚焦于国家作为价值法则的政治力量。作为一个税收国家，它完全依赖于国家财富的持续积累，因此也依赖于“股份”的国际竞争力。然而，国家的阶级性质并没有以民族术语来定义。它通过世界市场关系而存在。正如斯密所说，

股份的所有者本质上是世界的公民，并不必然依附于任何特定国家。如果他在一个国家受到繁琐的审查，为了重税而被评估，他会倾向于放弃这个国家，并将他的股份转移到他可以更轻松地开展业务或享受财富的其他国家。

在良好秩序的共同财富中，私有财产法律以物价机制重炮的形式显现，这种机制以“物资供应的便宜”为食，并因此不断施加压力以实现更大的劳动生产率，其便利化“产生了我们所说的警察”。

新自由主义与国家理论：关于自由经济和市场警察

认为新自由主义与国家弱化有关的传统观点与新自由主义对自由经济的概念几乎没有任何关系。对于新自由主义者来说，国家也是自由劳动社会不可或缺的政治力量。放任自流不是“对抗骚乱的答案”。对于新自由主义者来说，问题不在于国家是否应该干预。相反，问题在于干预的目的和方法，目标和意图。看不见的手的调节在经济领域是不可或缺的。如果任其自由发展，市场自由将“退化为粗俗的争执”（罗普克）在“贪婪的自私者”之间，面对无产阶级的破坏力量将自行崩溃（吕斯托）。它“不会产生社会融合”，也不会创造维持资产阶级社会所需的道德情感和企业家承诺。自由经济是一种政府实践。用哈耶克的话说，国家是“竞争的经济规划者”。它将（自由）经济视为一种超越司法体系和法治的政治上的有序自由的便利。根本上，经济自由取决于对经济自由的“意愿”（伯姆），这需要将竞争“融入到整体生活方式中”，如穆勒-阿马克所说，解除工人对资本主义自由的恐惧，使他们接受这种自由的自我责任。因此，需要国家来确保资本主义社会可支配的“心理道德力量”，将叛逆的无产者转变为劳动力的自我责任企业家。

新自由主义认识到，自由经济不是某种自动过程，它不是在行动个体之上客观展开或自我主张的过程。资本主义经济不包括“无条件有效的经济法则”，也不是由看不见的手的神学怪癖所控制，也不是依赖大众接受他们作为无产者生产剩余价值的存在而不进行斗争。工人被经济需要的沉闷强制支配的情形既不是那样自然发生的，也不是那样自然维持的。其现实既不是既定的，也不是有保障的。它是被创造的。也就是说，看不见的手的魔力依赖于不受限制的市场力量，而这又依赖于国家成功地消除对自由经济的阻碍。放任自流描述了所希望的经济条件。它不扩展到政治领域。事实上，哈耶克争论说，放任自流是“基于自由政策原则的一个高度模糊且容易引起误解的描述”。防止私人权力的“强制和暴力”，主要是通过组织劳动和无产者的自我组织，是一项政治任务。它旨在“彻底根除市场中的所有无序和消除经济中的私人权力”，确保社会个体作为经济价值的理性行为者，仅仅是价值增值的人类材料。国家与经济的制度性分离不仅仅是经济自由和社会自由的条件，即商品所有者之间非政治化的交换关系。相反，经济自由是一种政府实践。社会关系的非政治化不是经济自由的产物。总体而言，它相当于一种政府实践。自由经济假设不存在直接强制；劳动力的买卖者和剩余价值的生产者作为自由和平等的公民签订合同。因此，自由经济包含基于自我负责经济代理人之间合同的非政治化关系。直接的强制和暴力被经济偏好所取代，也就是说，实际上是经济强制的无声力量。这种交换关系的组织需要国家作为非政治化社会经济关系的力量。劳动力的贸易关系及其作为更大财富手段的有效利用相当于非政治化社会经济关系的政治实践。也就是说，自由经济“是一个极其政治性的决定”，需要一次又一次地做出，以包容自由的非自由使用和防止社会劳动关系的政治化。斯密的观点，即政治国家是看不见的手概念的固有部分，对新自由主义思想至关重要。它将国家视为看不见的手的公共权威，这是非政治化因素竞争和劳动力利用关系的调节手段。因此，集中和组织的强制力是自由经济的前提条件。等价交换的法则基于秩序，而建立秩序是一项政治事务。也就是说，看不见的手既不是“对既得利益饥饿群体”的答案，也不是对穷人的贪婪的答案，正如斯密所说。看不见的手基于国家作为“市场警察”。市场警察相当于不断努力确保自由经济的社会和道德前提条件。也就是说，总体而言，看不见的手的经济调节是一种政治实践。

请将“Liberalism therefore does not demand ‘weakness from the state, but only freedom for economic development under state protection’.58 In this sense, the state of the free economy does not really govern over society. Rather, it governs through the individuals. There is no freedom without the order of freedom, and order is not only a matter of law. It is also a matter of morality. The order of freedom entails surveillance as a means of freedom. The premise of government is that economic ‘security is only to be had at a price of constant watchfulness and adaptability and the preparedness of each individual to live courageously and put up with life’s insecurities’.59 There really is only one freedom, and that is the freedom of the self-responsible economic agents who adjust to the price signals with the will of and for enterprise, the one buying labour power with the expectation of making a profit, the other selling labour power as the dispossessed producer of surplus value, seeking to make ends meet. That is, poverty is neither unfreedom nor is it primarily material in character.60 Rather, poverty expresses a moral form of deprivation that is characterized by a poverty of aspiration, requiring state action to transform the sellers of labour power from quarrelsome proletarians into citizens of private property. As such a citizen the worker personifies labour power, which she takes to the market to trade for a wage. She appears thus as an entrepreneur of labour power, always ready to compete for a contract of employment. She thus perceives poverty as an incentive to do better, sees unemployment as an opportunity for employment, prices herself into jobs willingly and on her own initiative and takes her life into her own hands, gets on with things, lives courageously and puts up with life’s insecurities and risks. For the neoliberals, unemployed workers are fundamentally entrepreneurs of labour power in transit, ‘floating’ from one form of employment to another. However, the sociological condition of the worker is based on ‘the transformation of labour power into a commodity, which results from the separation of the worker from the means of production’.61 There is thus a ‘natural tendency towards proletarianisation’, and government is therefore required to counteract this tendency, time and time again, to secure the order of freedom.62 Government over society is government in and through society to ensure ‘the will’ for enterprise and labour market competition, integrating the free labourer into the capitalist relations of ‘coined freedom’ as a willing employer of labour power.63

In sum, the neoliberal conception of political economy develops Smith’s insight that the veracity of economic categories is a political one. That is, the ‘players in the game’ need to accept free economy, especially those who ‘might systematically do poorly’64 and who therefore struggle against a conceptuality of freedom that for them entails a double freedom: they are free from the means of subsistence and therefore free to produce surplus value in exchange for a wage to meet their subsistence needs. For the neoliberals, there is an innate connection between the law of private property and the order-making and order-preserving force of the state. Indeed, the organizational centre of political economy is the state, it is ‘the guardian of enterprise’,65 setting and enforcing the rules of the free and equal exchange relations between the sellers and buyers of commodities, comprising on the one side the owner of the means of production as the buyer of labour power, and on the other the producer of surplus value as the seller of labour power. Economy has no independent reality. It is a political economy.66





Political theology: Freedom and dictatorship

According to Carl Friedrich, the purpose of ‘military government is to protect the welfare of the governed’ – it is ‘inspired by humanitarian consideration’.67 However cynical, his take on the humanitarian character of military government expresses the obvious truth that, as the political form of bourgeois society, the state is responsible for maintaining the existing social relations. Friedrich recognizes that the institutional independence of the state from society is crucial to this task. It allows the state to function as a ‘strong and neutral guardian of the public interest’ asserting ‘its authority vis-à-vis the interest groups that press upon the government and clamour for recognition of their particular needs and wants’.68 His argument illustrates well Marx’s critique of the state as the executive committee of bourgeois society.

In our time, Milton Friedman has provided its cogent definition:

[T]he organisation of economic activity through voluntary exchange presumes that we have provided, through government, for the maintenance of law and order to prevent coercion of one individual by another, the enforcement of contracts voluntarily entered into, the definition of the meaning of property rights, the interpretation and enforcement of such rights, and the provision of a monetary framework.

The state has to ‘promote competition’ and do for the market what the market ‘cannot do for itself’. The state, then, is ‘essential both as a forum for determining the “rules of the game” and as an umpire to interpret and enforce the rules decided upon’ and enforcement is necessary ‘on the part of those few who would otherwise not play the game’. The state, he says, is the means through which ‘we’ set and modify the rules.69 However, what happens when ‘they’ fail to adjust to the demands of the market and instead start to rebel? Rebellions cannot be suppressed by ‘juridical injunction’.70 Suppression requires the employment of concentrated force that ‘in the name of freedom’ is said to protect the welfare of the governed. At a time of liberal emergency, the occasion might thus arise when ‘the law needs to be broken in order to preserve it’.71 The law does not suspend itself. Suspension is an eminently political decision.

In Carl Schmitt’s definition of the political as the relationship between enemy and foe the state is properly a state on the condition that it recognizes the class enemy and formulates and conducts its policies on the basis of this recognition. He sees the state as the ‘monopolist of the ultimate decision’ as to whether the containment of the enemy on the basis of the rule of law is effective or whether its temporary suspension is required so that order can be restored for the sake of sound economy under the rule of law.72 He argued that ‘there is no legal norm that can be applied to chaos. Order has to be established for the legal norms to be effective’.73 Whether there is order or disorder, whether the rule of law applies or whether the force of law (making violence) is needed to reassert the rule of law is a question of judgement – not of law but of a sovereign decision. The decision is valid because it has been made. For the decision to be valid there is thus need to eliminate any doubt in the veracity of the action. All law, as Schmitt put it, is ‘situational’ – not as an expression of a shifting balance of the social forces that in the view of Jessop act through the state, defining its character and purpose.74 Here the rule of law reflects the ‘situation’ of the balance of class forces. For Schmitt, the rule of law is situational in character because its veracity is a matter of authoritative judgement about the conditions of social order. Society is either governed by the rule of law or it is not, in which case government by the force of law-making violence becomes necessary to restore the liberal veracity of the rule of law. The rule of law is thus situational because it depends on the sovereign decision as to whether it applies or not. Schmitt thus denounces the tradition of political pluralism and legal positivism as doctrines based on the idea of relative truth. Normative values, he argues, are either absolute or they posit nothing at all. Thus, ‘relative truth never gives one the courage to use force and to spill blood’.75 Whether the rule of law applies or whether it needs to be suspended is thus a question, not of law, but of sovereign decision and judgement in the face of the (class) enemy. Necessity knows no law. The law of necessity is the law of violence. ‘Sovereign is who decides on the state of emergency.’76

Long before Schmitt, Rosa Luxemburg’s critique of the state saw clearly what Schmitt affirms. In the context of the debate between the revolutionary and reformist wings of German social democracy, she declared that the state ‘is itself the representative of capitalist society. It is a class state’.77 It recognizes bourgeois class interests as universal-human interests, and governs accordingly.

In this society, the representative institutions, democratic in form, are in content the instrument of the interests of the ruling class. This manifests itself in a tangible fashion in the fact that as soon as democracy shows the tendency to negate its class character and become transformed into an instrument of the real interests of the population, the democratic forms are sacrificed by the bourgeoisie and by its state representatives.78

Schumpeter thus argued that at its best liberal democracy is a most effective form of government because it allows for the peaceful circulation of elites by means of competitive elections.79 The great danger for the democratic state is the democratization of society.80 By recognizing and organizing its own powers directly as social powers, the democratization of the society challenges the distinction between society and state by politicizing the social relations, leading to conditions of ungovernability, if unchecked. For the sake of liberty, the state needs thus to be rolled back from society, reinforcing the distinction between state and society and thus securing the political state as the presupposition of a depoliticized society, one that as a consequence of the political effort of socio-economic depoliticization, is solely governed by the liberal rule of law and the moral sentiments of enterprise and competition.

Reasserting the distinction between state and society is an eminently political decision with potentially far-reaching consequences.81 Rossiter therefore argues that for the sake of liberal democracy ‘no sacrifice is too great for our democracy, least of all the temporary sacrifice of democracy itself’.82 Rossiter’s point focuses the raison d’être of Bonapartism succinctly. Bonapartism is the theology of political reaction. It asks what is necessary to secure bourgeois interests as the universal interests of society at a time of manifest political crisis. Its political force comes to the fore when the ‘constitutional government must be temporarily altered to whatever degree is necessary to overcome the peril and restore normal conditions’.83 That is, the democratization of society creates, as Friedrich put it, ‘the states of emergency which call for the establishment of constitutional dictatorship’.84 Necessity knows no law. It demands the use of force to restore the order of the common wealth. For Schmitt, therefore, an effective democracy depends on the fundamental homogeneity between rulers and ruled. It is, as it were, possible only on the condition that it is a democracy of ‘friends’.85”翻译为中文

因此，自由主义并不要求国家“软弱”，而只要求在国家保护下的经济发展自由。从这个意义上说，自由经济的国家并不真正统治社会。相反，它通过个体治理。没有自由的秩序就没有自由，而秩序不仅仅是法律的问题。它也是道德的问题。自由的秩序包含了作为自由手段的监视。政府的前提是经济“安全只能通过不断的警惕、适应性和每个人的勇气生活以及忍受生活的不安全”来获得。真正只有一种自由，那就是自我负责的经济主体的自由，他们根据价格信号并带有创业意愿和目的进行调整，一方买入劳动力期望获利，另一方作为被剥夺的剩余价值生产者出售劳动力，试图维持生计。也就是说，贫困既不是不自由，也主要不是物质性的。相反，贫困表达了一种道德形式的剥夺，其特征是抱负的贫乏，需要国家行动将劳动力的卖者从好争斗的无产者转变为私有财产的公民。作为这样的公民，工人体现了劳动力，她带着劳动力去市场进行交易以换取工资。她因此表现为劳动力的企业家，始终准备竞争就业合同。她因此将贫困视为做得更好的激励，将失业视为就业的机会，愿意并主动给自己定价进入工作岗位，并主宰自己的生活，勇敢地生活并忍受生活的不确定性和风险。对于新自由主义者来说，失业工人根本上是处于过渡中的劳动力企业家，“浮动”于一种就业形式到另一种就业形式。然而，工人的社会条件基于“劳动力转化为商品，这是由工人与生产资料分离而产生的”。因此存在“向无产化的自然倾向”，因此需要政府一次又一次地采取行动来对抗这种倾向，以确保自由的秩序。在和通过社会的政府是为了确保创业和劳动力市场竞争的“意愿”，将自由劳工整合到“铸造自由”的资本主义关系中，成为愿意聘用劳动力的雇主。

总之，新自由主义的政治经济学概念发展了斯密的洞见，即经济范畴的真实性是政治性的。也就是说，“游戏中的参与者”需要接受自由经济，尤其是那些“可能系统性地表现不佳”的人，因此他们反对对他们而言意味着双重自由的自由概念：他们从维持生计的手段中解放出来，因此自由地以换取维持生计所需的工资来生产剩余价值。对于新自由主义者来说，私有财产法律与国家的秩序制定和维护力量之间存在内在联系。事实上，政治经济学的组织中心是国家，它是“企业的守护者”，制定和执行商品买卖双方之间自由平等交换关系的规则，一方是作为劳动力购买者的生产资料所有者，另一方是作为劳动力卖者的剩余价值生产者。经济没有独立的现实。它是一种政治经济学。

政治神学：自由与独裁

根据卡尔·弗里德里希的说法，“军事政府的目的是保护被统治者的福祉”——它“受到人道主义考虑的启发”。然而他对军事政府人道特性的看法不管多么讽刺，都表达了一个明显的真理，即作为资产阶级社会的政治形式，国家负责维护现存的社会关系。弗里德里希认识到，国家与社会的机构独立对于这一任务至关重要。它允许国家作为“公共利益的强大而中立的守护者”发挥作用，对那些向政府施压并为其特定需求和欲望的认可而喧嚣的利益团体表现出“其权威”。他的论点很好地说明了马克思对国家作为资产阶级社会执行委员会的批判。

在我们这个时代，米尔顿·弗里德曼提供了其明确的定义：

通过政府提供的方式组织经济活动的自愿交换假定我们已经为维护法律和秩序以防止一个人对另一个人的强制、执行自愿订立的合同、定义财产权的含义、解释和执行这些权利以及提供货币框架。

国家必须“促进竞争”并为市场做市场“无法自行完成”的事情。因此，国家是“确定‘游戏规则’的论坛和解释及执行所决定规则的裁判员”，并且对那些少数不愿参与游戏的人进行强制是必要的。他说，国家是“我们”设定和修改规则的手段。然而，当“他们”未能适应市场的要求而开始反抗时会发生什么？叛乱不能通过“法律禁令”被镇压。镇压需要使用集中的力量，“以自由的名义”据称保护被统治者的福祉。在自由主义紧急状态下，可能会出现“为了保护法律而需要打破法律”的情形。法律不会自我暂停。暂停是一个极度政治性的决定。

在卡尔·施密特将政治定义为敌我关系的定义中，只有国家认识到阶级敌人并根据这一认识制定并执行其政策，国家才是真正的国家。他将国家视为对于是否基于法治有效遏制敌人或是否需要其临时暂停以恢复秩序从而为法治下的健全经济服务的“最终决定的垄断者”。他认为，“没有法律规范可以应用于混乱。必须建立秩序才能使法律规范生效”。是否存在秩序或混乱，是否适用法治或是否需要法治力量（制定法律的暴力）来重新确认法治，这是一个判断问题——不是法律问题，而是主权决定问题。决定之所以有效，是因为它已经做出。因此，为了确保决定的有效性，需要消除对行动真实性的任何怀疑。正如施密特所说，所有法律都是“情境性的”——不是作为杰索普所说的通过国家行动的社会力量变化平衡的表达。在这里，法治反映了阶级力量平衡的“情境”。对于施密特来说，法治之所以具有情境性特征，是因为其真实性是关于社会秩序状况的权威性判断。社会要么由法治治理，要么不是，在这种情况下，需要通过制定法律的暴力治理来恢复自由法治的真实性。因此，法治之所以具有情境性，是因为它取决于是否适用的主权决定。因此，施密特否定了政治多元主义和法律实证主义的传统，将其视为基于相对真理观念的学说。他辩称，规范价值要么是绝对的，要么根本就没有提出任何东西。因此，“相对真理从不给人使用武力和流血的勇气”。因此，法治是否适用或是否需要暂停，这不是一个法律问题，而是一个主权决定和判断的问题，面对（阶级）敌人。必要性不认识法律。必要性法则是暴力法则。“主权者是决定紧急状态的人。”

早在施密特之前，罗莎·卢森堡对国家的批判清楚地看到了施密特所确认的内容。在德国社会民主党革命派和改良派之间的辩论中，她宣称国家“本身就是资本主义社会的代表。它是一个阶级国家”。它将资产阶级阶级利益视为普遍人类利益，并据此治理。

在这个社会中，民主形式的代表机构在内容上是统治阶级利益的工具。这在一个具体的事实中得到清晰的表现，即一旦民主倾向于否定其阶级性质并转变为真正人民利益的工具时，民主形式就会被资产阶级及其国家代表牺牲。

舒姆彼特因此认为，在最佳情况下，自由民主是一种最有效的政府形式，因为它允许通过竞争性选举和平地更替精英。民主国家的巨大危险在于社会的民主化。通过直接识别和组织自己的力量作为社会力量，社会的民主化挑战了社会与国家之间的区别，通过将社会关系政治化导致不可治理的状态，如果不加以检查。为了自由，因此需要将国家从社会中撤回，加强国家与社会之间的区别，从而确保政治国家作为非政治化社会的前提条件，这种社会作为社会经济非政治化的政治努力的结果，仅仅由自由法治和企业与竞争的道德情感治理。

重新确立国家和社会之间的区别是一个极其政治性的决定，可能产生深远的后果。因此，罗斯特尔认为，为了自由民主，“为我们的民主牺牲任何东西都是值得的，尤其是民主本身的暂时牺牲”。罗斯特尔的观点简洁地集中了波拿巴主义的存在理由。波拿巴主义是政治反应的神学。它询问在明显的政治危机时期，为了确保资产阶级利益作为社会的普遍利益，需要采取什么措施。当“宪法政府必须暂时改变以克服危险并恢复正常状态”时，其政治力量就显现出来。也就是说，社会的民主化创造了，正如弗里德里希所说，“呼唤建立宪法独裁的紧急状态”。必要性不认识法律。它要求使用武力恢复共同财富的秩序。因此，对于施密特来说，有效的民主依赖于统治者和被统治者之间的根本同质性。它只有在作为“朋友”的民主的条件下才是可能的。

请将“Schmitt’s programme of ‘sound economy and the strong state’ is the demand of neoliberalism.86 For the sake of an economy of free labour, liberalism has to put itself at the ‘forefront of the fight for the state’ because it is only the state that can guarantee the ‘common wealth’, and liberalism should not involve itself with defending particular interests. It should always focus on the ‘whole’, and this whole ‘is the state’.87 The purpose of ‘dictatorship within the bounds of democracy’ is innately social and moral as social disorder can only be resolved by a strong state that suppresses the class struggle.88 Röpke defines this ‘dictatorship within the bounds of democracy’ correctly as a commissarial dictatorship.89 It temporarily suspends the rule of law to restore legitimate authority in the face of an ‘extreme emergency’, for which he holds responsible those who lack the ‘moral stamina’ to absorb economic shocks.90 The neoliberal demand for the strong state is a demand for the limited state, one that limits itself to the task of making the economy of free labour effective. In the face of disorder and politicized labour relations, the state has to act and when it has to act ‘the most fundamental principles of a free society . . . may have to be temporarily sacrificed . . . [to preserve] liberty in the long run’.91 According to Hayek, ‘dictatorship may impose limits on itself, and a dictatorship that imposes such limits may be more liberal in its policies than a democratic assembly that knows of no such limits’.92 Unsurprisingly, Hayek accepts Schmitt’s conception of sovereignty – ‘sovereign is the one who decides on the exception’.93 A dictatorship that imposes limits on itself, and that thereby governs for the sake of the free economy, is a dictatorship that ‘should be no cause for alarm’ because it is ‘constitutional’.94 That is to say, a constitutional dictatorship is not ‘a contradiction in terms’ but rather ‘the litmus test of constitutionalism’. In the face of liberal emergency, it preserves the rule of law by means of the force of law.95 Rossiter puts this point succinctly when he argues that ‘law is made for the state, not the state for the law’. In case circumstances ‘are such that a choice must be made between the two, it is the law which needs be sacrificed to the state’.96

The use of ‘organised force’, which according to Friedrich is employed for the welfare of the governed, does not entail less liberty. On the contrary, it is the condition of liberty. It preserves and sustains the prospects of liberty by means of ‘executive action’. The ‘extraordinary means for maintaining the state’97 – from martial law to a state of emergency, from restraint of civil liberties to a full-blown constitutional or commissarial dictatorship – entail not just a temporary strengthening of the state where ‘government will have more power, the people fewer rights’.98 Fundamentally, it is a means of ‘freedom’.99 Nevertheless, there is the danger that dictatorship becomes ‘totalitarian’, or in the words of Schmitt, instead of the desired commissarial dictatorship, it assumes the character of a sovereign dictatorship. What to do? As Friedrich put it, ‘how are we to get effective, vigorous governmental action and yet limit the power of governmental bodies so as to forestall the rise of a despotic concentration of power?’100 For Friedrich this is a ‘logical paradox’ that can only be resolved in practice, that is, ‘whatever forbidden fields of freedom the necessities of crisis may force the leaders of a constitutional government to go, go they must’.101

In sum, the point of political theology is not to provide a political theory of the state, let alone a critique of the state as the political form of capitalist social relations. The point of political theology is to ask what is necessary to sustain the existent social relations. It moves, as it were, from the lectern to the barracks, seeking to maintain the economy of free labour in the face of a manifest social crisis, class conflict and political strife. It is premised on the understanding that economic liberty amounts to a political practice of ‘market police’.





Conclusion: On the critique of political economy

The tradition of political economy establishes the state as the political form of free economy. It conceives of the state as the political force of the invisible hand. Without government, society descends into ‘disorder and bloodshed’. Government is the condition and premise of bourgeois society, civilizing its antagonistic character. It maintains the unity of the disunited social relations, and contains class antagonism on the basis of law and morality, and by means of force. The political state is the forceful premise of the relations of liberty, equality and utility. The difference between the neoliberals and Smith is that unlike Smith, they do not provide a social theory of capitalist social relations. They demand strong state action as the means of preserving and sustaining free economy. Economic regulation by the free price mechanism manifests a constant political effort of facilitating economic freedom in the social structure and moral fabric of society. A free labour economy presupposes the state as the political authority of that freedom. In its role as market police, the state is fundamentally a security state, ever vigilant in its surveillance of society to secure the proper use of freedom, ‘policing’ not only compliance with the rule of law but also the will for enterprise. Whether there is disorder or order is a matter, not of law but political decision. Their political stance expresses the ‘Bonapartist’ theology of state power, according to which the veracity of the strong state is not relative. It is absolute in its pursuit of a free economy of labour. Smith, in contrast, does not even utter the word ‘liberalism’. There was no such system to defend. Smith wrote his work as a critique of mercantilism. By the beginning of the nineteenth century, liberalism had become the ideological orthodoxy of a liberalizing state.102 It was in this context that Marx writes (with Engels) in the Communist Manifesto about the cosmopolitan character of the bourgeoisie, and defines the national state as the executive committee of the bourgeoisie.

The idea that the economy comprises an independent reality expresses a theological conviction. The capitalist state is neither independent from the economy nor does it derive from it, nor does the economy comprise a structured system of independent economic laws. Capitalist economy is a socially constituted system of human reproduction that is antagonistic from the outset. Its cohesion, organization, integration and reproduction are matters of state. It provides for the ‘organisation of economy activity’.103 The tradition of political economy does not therefore define the state by its relationship to the market, nor does it define the economic in relationship to the state, a view which implies a conception of market and state as two distinct modes of social organization, and the perennial question about such a conception is whether the market has autonomy vis-à-vis the state or conversely whether the state has autonomy vis-à-vis the maket, characterizing its retreat or resurgence as a power vis-à-vis the economic. For political economy the relationship between the economy and state is an innate one, and within their ‘inner connection’, the state is fundamental. It manifests the free economy of labour as a practice of government.

There can thus be no economic crisis. Economic crises are crises of political economy. In Röpke’s view one should therefore ‘not speak of a “crisis of capitalism” but of a “crisis of interventionism”’.104 That is, the state is ‘the mistress of the economy in its totality as in its parts . . . and the state must master the whole of economic development both intellectually and materially’; it must plan for liberty and competition.105 Economic crises therefore manifest a failure of government, which turned a blind eye to the problems of economy, society and morality. The state failed either in removing impediments to market freedom or in restraining the passions of competition or greed, or in securing the cheapness of provision, or in facilitating the requisite psycho-moral forces of enterprise, or in embedding the spirit of enterprise into society at large, or compounded the poverty of aspirations by a permissive welfare state, or turned a blind eye to the rapacity of the poor, which could have been curbed by means of welfare concessions, employment programmes, etc. The ‘failure’ of the state is the failure of the executive committee of bourgeois society. Marx’s conception of the state as the concentrated and organized force of society – ‘of society viewed in relationship to itself’ – focuses political economy as a political practice. As the organized force of the system of wealth based on free labour the state facilitates the order of economic freedom, sustains the capitalist relations of production and exchange, and seeks the further progress of the system of free labour by facilitating the ‘cheapness of provision’.106

Marx’s stance elucidates the liberal paradox that the state always governs too much and too little, commits too much welfare support or too little, regulates too much or too little. The market liberal idea that uninhibited market forces are the only remedy to resolving economic crisis is deceitful. Uninhibited markets describe a political practice of eliminating impediments to free economy. Similarly, the idea that market failure is a consequence of too little or too much regulation is deceitful in that it identifies the cause of crises in the adopted means of economic regulation, as if crisis resolution is a matter of technical fine-tuning. Nevertheless, in either case, the state is endorsed as the indispensable force of capitalist social reproduction. For the sake of the capitalist form of wealth, of value in process, money in process, and as such capital, the state cannot have enough power – the law of value presupposes the ‘political state’ (Marx) as the concentrated force of the relations of bourgeois freedom. In the German Ideology, Marx sketches the character of the bourgeois relations of reproduction as follows: everyone is dependent on everyone else, and each person can only reproduce himself inasmuch as all others become means for him. Furthermore, each individual can only pursue and realize his own particular interests when his conditions of reproduction, which are identical to those of everyone else, are accepted, respected and recognized by everyone else. The particular will of the individual obtains thus through a will in which all individuals are united, which is common to all, that is, is universal as the bonum commune of their sociability as personifications of value. The ‘attitude of the bourgeois to the conditions of his existence acquires one of its universal forms in bourgeois morality’ and these forms ‘must assume the form of the state’ and must gain ‘expression as the will of the state, as law’. That is to say, it is ‘precisely because individuals who are independent of one another assert themselves and their own will, and because on this basis their attitude to one another is bound to be egotistical, that self-denial is made necessary in law and right’ and assumes the form of the state.107”翻译为中文

施密特的“健全经济和强大国家”计划是新自由主义的要求。为了自由劳动的经济，自由主义必须将自己置于“为国家而战的最前线”，因为只有国家才能保证“共同财富”，自由主义不应卷入捍卫特定利益。它应始终关注“整体”，而这个整体“就是国家”。“民主限度内的独裁”的目的从内在上是社会性和道德性的，因为社会混乱只能由一个强大的国家通过压制阶级斗争来解决。罗普克正确地将这种“民主限度内的独裁”定义为委员独裁。它暂时暂停法治以在面对“极端紧急情况”时恢复合法权威，对此，他认为那些缺乏“道德坚韧性”来吸收经济冲击的人应负责。新自由主义对强大国家的要求是对有限国家的要求，一种将自己限制在使自由劳动经济有效的任务上的国家。面对混乱和政治化的劳动关系，国家必须采取行动，当它必须采取行动时，“一个自由社会的最根本原则......可能不得不暂时牺牲......为了长期保持自由”。根据哈耶克的说法，“独裁可能会对自身设限，而在其政策上可能比不认识这种限制的民主议会更自由”。不出所料，哈耶克接受了施密特的主权观念——“主权者是决定例外状态的人”。对自身设限的独裁，并因此为了自由经济而治理的独裁，“不应引起恐慌”，因为它是“宪法性”的。也就是说，宪法独裁并不是“用词矛盾”，而是“宪政主义的试金石”。面对自由主义紧急情况，它通过法律的力量保持法治。罗西特在这一点上表述得很简洁，他认为“法律是为国家而设，不是国家为了法律”。如果情况“如此，必须在两者之间做出选择，那么需要牺牲的是法律以保全国家”。

使用“有组织的力量”，根据弗里德里希的说法，是为了被统治者的福祉而采用的，并不意味着减少自由。相反，它是自由的条件。它通过“行政行动”保护并维持自由的前景。“维护国家的特殊手段”——从戒严到紧急状态，从限制公民自由到全面的宪法或委员独裁——不仅仅是国家暂时加强的表现，即“政府将拥有更多权力，人民的权利更少”。根本上，它是“自由”的手段。然而，存在独裁变成“极权主义”的危险，或用施密特的话说，而不是期望的委员独裁，它呈现为主权独裁的特性。该怎么办？正如弗里德里希所说，“我们如何才能获得有效、有力的政府行动，同时又限制政府机构的权力以防止权力的暴政集中？”对于弗里德里希来说，这是一个“逻辑悖论”，只能在实践中得到解决，即“无论宪政政府的领袖们因危机的需要而被迫进入何种禁区，他们都必须前进”。

总之，政治神学的要点不是提供一个关于国家的政治理论，更不用说批判国家作为资本主义社会关系的政治形式了。政治神学的要点是询问为了维持现有社会关系所需的是什么。它从讲台转向兵营，试图在面临明显的社会危机、阶级冲突和政治斗争时维持自由劳动的经济。它基于理解，即经济自由相当于“市场警察”的政治实践。

结论：关于政治经济学的批判

政治经济学传统将国家确立为自由经济的政治形式。它将国家视为看不见的手的政治力量。没有政府，社会就会陷入“混乱和流血”。政府是资产阶级社会的条件和前提，使其对立性质文明化。它维护着不团结的社会关系的统一，并在法律和道德的基础上以及通过力量包含阶级对立。政治国家是自由、平等和效用关系的有力前提。新自由主义者和斯密之间的区别在于，与斯密不同，他们没有提供资本主义社会关系的社会理论。他们要求强有力的国家行动作为维护和维持自由经济的手段。通过自由价格机制的经济调节体现了在社会结构和道德结构中促进经济自由的不断政治努力。自由劳动经济假设国家是那种自由的政治权威。作为市场警察，国家根本上是一个安全国家，始终在监视社会以确保自由的正确使用，“警察”不仅遵守法治，而且有创业的意愿。存在混乱还是秩序，不是法律问题，而是政治决定问题。他们的政治立场表达了“波拿巴式”国家权力神学，根据这一神学，强大国家的真实性不是相对的。它在追求基于自由劳动的自由经济方面是绝对的。与斯密相比，斯密甚至没有提到“自由主义”这个词。那时还没有这样的体系需要捍卫。斯密撰写他的作品是作为对重商主义的批判。到了19世纪初，自由主义已经成为自由化国家的意识形态正统。就在这个背景下，马克思在《共产党宣言》中写道，资产阶级具有世界主义特征，并将民族国家定义为资产阶级的执行委员会。

认为经济包含一个独立现实的观念表达了一种神学信念。资本主义国家既不独立于经济，也不是从经济衍生出来的，经济也不包含一套独立的经济法则。资本主义经济是一种从一开始就具有对立性的社会再生产体系。其凝聚、组织、整合和再生产是国家的事务。它为“经济活动的组织”提供了条件。因此，政治经济学传统并不是通过市场与国家之间的关系来定义国家，也不是通过国家与经济之间的关系来定义经济，这种观点暗示了市场和国家作为两种不同的社会组织模式的概念，而对这种概念的永恒问题是，市场是否对国家具有自主性，反之亦然，是否国家对市场具有自主性，表征其作为经济力量相对于经济的撤退或复兴。对于政治经济学来说，经济与国家之间的关系是内在的，在他们的“内在联系”中，国家是基础。它体现了自由劳动经济作为一种政府实践。

因此，不可能存在经济危机。经济危机是政治经济学的危机。按照罗普克的观点，因此“不应该谈论‘资本主义危机’，而应该谈论‘干预主义危机’”。也就是说，国家是“作为整体及其部分经济的主人......国家必须在智力和物质上掌握整个经济发展”；它必须为自由和竞争进行规划。因此，经济危机表现为政府的失败，它对经济、社会和道德问题视而不见。国家未能消除对市场自由的障碍，未能抑制竞争或贪婪的激情，未能保证物资供应的便宜，或未能促进企业所需的心理道德力量，或未能将企业精神融入整个社会，或通过放任的福利国家加剧了抱负的贫困，或对穷人的贪婪视而不见，这本可以通过福利让步、就业计划等手段加以遏制。国家的“失败”是资产阶级社会执行委员会的失败。马克思将国家视为“集中和组织化的社会力量——‘关于它自身的社会的关系’”的概念，聚焦政治经济学作为一种政治实践。作为基于自由劳动的财富体系的组织力量，国家促进经济自由的秩序，维持资本主义的生产和交换关系，并通过促进“物资供应的便宜”寻求自由劳动体系的进一步进步。

马克思的立场阐明了自由主义的悖论，即国家总是管得太多和太少，提供太多或太少的福利支持，过度或不足地进行管制。市场自由主义观念认为，不受限制的市场力量是解决经济危机的唯一方法，这是一种欺骗性的观念。不受限制的市场描述了一种消除对自由经济障碍的政治实践。同样，市场失败是由于管制过少或过多所致的观念也是欺骗性的，因为它将危机的原因归咎于采用的经济调节手段，就好像危机解决是一种技术性微调的问题。然而，在任何一种情况下，国家都被认可为资本主义社会再生产的不可或缺力量。为了基于自由劳动的财富形式，即处于过程中的价值、货币和作为资本的价值，国家无法拥有足够的权力——价值法则假设“政治国家”（马克思）作为资产阶级自由关系的集中力量。在《德意志意识形态》中，马克思勾勒了资产阶级再生产关系的特征如下：每个人都依赖于其他人，而每个人只能在所有其他人成为他的手段的情况下才能实现自我再生产。此外，每个人只有在他的再生产条件被所有其他人接受、尊重和认可的情况下，才能追求并实现自己的特定利益。个体的特定意愿因此通过所有个体团结起来的意愿获得，这是共同的、普遍的、作为价值体现者的社会性的善共同体。资产阶级社会再生产条件的“资产阶级道德”态度在它的普遍形式之一中达到了表现，“必须以国家的形式呈现，并作为国家的意志、作为法律得到表达”。也就是说，“正是因为相互独立的个体主张自己及其意志，因为在这个基础上，他们彼此之间的态度必然是自私的，因此在法律和权利中的自我否定变得必要”，并采取国家的形式。

请将“The rule of law treats the owners of the means of production and the free labourer as identical subjects, as citizens endowed with the same legal rights and obligations. The law is blind to privileges. It is a law of equality. Contractual relations represent the form in which, according to law, freedom obtains in the form of a legally bound recognition of private individuals in their relationship to one another. The labour contract is the juridical form of the bourgeois freedom of labour – it combines the freedom of exchange on the labour market with the acquired right to compel the worker beyond the necessary labour time and appropriate her unpaid labour time in the form of profit. This then characterizes the form of the state as an ‘illusory community’.108 In this community of equals ‘each pays heed to himself only, and no one worries about the rest. And precisely for that reason, either in accordance with the pre-established harmony of things, or under the auspices of an omniscient providence, they all work together to their mutual advantage, for the common weal and in the common interest.’109 Marx’s ironic take on the magic of the invisible hand as the regulative principle of bourgeois freedom, entails the understanding that in this society the individuals are indeed governed by real economic abstractions. However, these abstractions have no independent reality, they are socially constituted abstractions. Similarly the state is not an independent power. Rather it is ‘society’s [independent] power’.110 The state, then, does not ‘possesses its own intellectual, ethical and libertarian bases’.111 Rather, society doubles itself up into society and state. Instead of despotism, the state of capitalist society imposes law on the basis of order; it is the concentrated force of social order. It makes order by means of the force of law-making violence. It depoliticizes the socio-economic relations and so guarantees contractual relations of social interaction, secures the free and equal market relations and maintains the social relations founded on equality, freedom and utility.

The depoliticization of society entails the ‘concentrating of the political character’ of society in the form of the state, this independent institutional form that is the concentrated force of society. The political form does not derive from bourgeois society. Rather it is immanent to its concept. For the sake of liberty, it forbids the dependent sellers of labour power and the owners of the means of subsistence equally to steal bread. The political state is the state of bourgeois society.
”翻译为中文

法治将生产资料所有者和自由劳工视为同一主体，视为享有相同法律权利和义务的公民。法律对特权视而不见。它是平等的法律。合约关系代表了按照法律，自由以私人个体之间的法定认可形式获得的形式。劳动合同是资产阶级劳动自由的法律形式——它结合了劳动力市场上的交换自由以及强迫工人超出必要劳动时间并以利润形式占有其未付劳动时间的获得权利。这样就描绘了国家作为“虚幻社区”的形态。在这个平等者的社区中，“每个人只关注自己，没有人担心其他人。而正因为如此，要么根据事物的预设和谐，要么在全知的天意庇佑下，他们都相互合作以实现共同利益，为了共同福祉和共同利益。”马克思对看不见的手作为资产阶级自由的调节原则的讽刺态度，包含了这样的理解：在这个社会中，个体确实受到真实经济抽象概念的统治。然而，这些抽象概念没有独立的现实，它们是社会构成的抽象概念。同样，国家不是一个独立的权力。相反，它是“社会的[独立的]力量”。因此，国家并不“拥有自己的智力、伦理和自由的基础”。相反，社会将自身分为社会和国家。资本主义社会的国家不是基于专制，而是基于秩序施加法律；它是社会秩序的集中力量。它通过制定法律的暴力手段创造秩序。它使社会经济关系去政治化，从而保证社会互动的合约关系，确保自由平等的市场关系，并维持建立在平等、自由和效用基础上的社会关系。

社会的去政治化需要将社会的政治特征“集中”于国家的形式，这种独立的制度形式是社会的集中力量。政治形态不是由资产阶级社会衍生而来。相反，它内在于其概念。为了自由，它同样禁止依赖出售劳动力的卖方和维持生计手段的所有者偷面包。政治国家是资产阶级社会的国家。
